conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me review the official eligibility criteria to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, **do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me:
- How many children you're inquiring about?
- Their ages (or approximate ages)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who's 21 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I can see that Alex is 21 years old and lives at home with you.

To determine eligibility, I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

(Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they're in approved education or training, but once a child turns 20, they would no longer qualify for Child Benefit.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Based on what you've told me, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me explain the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: Child Benefit is available for children under 16, or for young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 years old, they have exceeded the maximum age limit for Child Benefit, regardless of whether they're in education or training or not.

Child Benefit would have stopped when Alex either:
- Turned 20, if they were in approved education or training, or
- Turned 16 and left education or training

Is there anything else I can help you with regarding benefits or your circumstances?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 21 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
circumstances: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 21 years old and is not in approved education or training. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you'll still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 years old, they have exceeded the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility, even if they were in approved education or training. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 21 years old and not in education or training. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or for those aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21, they exceed the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 21 years old and is not in approved education or training. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you'll still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 years old, they have exceeded the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility, even if they were in approved education or training. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 21 years old and not in education or training. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or for those aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21, they exceed the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility.
